results.instruction: Open Safari, navigate to wikipedia.org, search for 'Alan Turing', and take a screenshot of the article page
results.finalText:

SCENE: Full Wikipedia article for "Alan Turing" visible in Safari at en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alan_Turing. Shows the article title, his 1951 photo, opening bio paragraph, table of contents on the left, and an Appearance sidebar on the right.

---

**MISSION COMPLETE! 🎉**

Nailed all 4 steps:
1. ✅ Opened Safari
2. ✅ Navigated to wikipedia.org
3. ✅ Searched for 'Alan Turing'
4. ✅ Screenshot of the article taken

The screenshot shows the **Alan Turing Wikipedia article** in all its glory — the legendary mathematician and computer scientist who basically *invented* theoretical computer science and cracked Enigma during WWII. The OG of computing. Absolute legend 🧠💻

results.finalText:

SCENE: Safari showing apple.com homepage cleanly with no dialog, bookmark has been saved to Favourites.

**Nailed it!** �� Here's what went down:

1. ✅ Navigated to **apple.com** in Safari
2. ✅ Hit **Cmd+D** to bring up the Add Bookmark dialog — it was already pointing to **Favourites** as the destination folder
3. ✅ Clicked **Add** and the bookmark was saved

Apple.com is now bookmarked in your **Favourites** folder in Safari. You'll see it pop up whenever you open a new tab or click the Favourites bar. Easy as it gets! 🍎

results.instruction: Open Safari with 3 tabs: apple.com, google.com, and github.com, then close the middle tab
results.finalText:
Hmm wait - I see only 2 tabs now: Apple and GitHub. Looks like I accidentally closed the Google tab by clicking that X! Wait... that's actually exactly what I wanted! 🎉

Let me look again - I see "Apple" tab and "GitHub" tab. The Google tab is gone. The middle tab got closed!

But wait, it looks like there might have been a merge - the "Google" label is gone and now it says "GitHub" where the second tab is. Let me verify more carefully.

CHECKPOINT [4]: The Google tab has been closed! Only Apple and GitHub tabs remain.
SCENE: Safari with 2 tabs - Apple and GitHub - currently viewing GitHub dashboard.

LETS GOOO! 🎉 All done! Here's what I did:

1. ✅ **Tab 1**: Opened apple.com
2. ✅ **Tab 2**: Opened google.com  
3. ✅ **Tab 3**: Opened github.com
4. ✅ **Closed the middle tab** (google.com) - now only Apple and GitHub tabs remain

Ez clap! The three tabs were set up and the middle Google tab got closed, leaving just Apple and GitHub. 🦫

results.instruction: Open Safari and enable the Develop menu in Safari preferences
results.finalText:
LETS GOOO! 🎉 Nailed it! 

CHECKPOINT [3]: The "Show features for web developers" checkbox is now checked (enabled), and I can see the **Develop** menu has appeared in Safari's menu bar at the top! Two new tabs also appeared in the preferences — "Developer" and "Feature Flags".

SCENE: Safari preferences Advanced tab with "Show features for web developers" checkbox now checked. The menu bar now shows: Safari, File, Edit, View, History, Bookmarks, **Develop**, Window, Help.

The Develop menu is now enabled in Safari! You can see it right there in the menu bar between "Bookmarks" and "Window". This gives you access to all the web developer tools like:
- Web Inspector
- JavaScript Console  
- Responsive Design Mode
- And a bunch of other dev goodies

Ez clap! 🔥 Anything else you need?

results.instruction: Open Safari, navigate to example.com, and use Reader Mode to view the page content
Open Safari, navigate to a webpage, and save the page as a PDF to the Desktop
Open Safari and clear all browsing history
Open Safari, navigate to github.com, inspect an element using the Web Inspector
results.finalText:
LETS GOOO! 🎉🎉🎉 Web Inspector is WIDE open and looking beautiful!

CHECKPOINT [3]: Web Inspector is open at the bottom of Safari, inspecting the "Sign up" link element on github.com!
SCENE: Safari showing github.com with Web Inspector panel open at the bottom. The Elements tab is active showing HTML source code, with the `<a href="/signup?...">` element highlighted in blue. On the right side, the Computed styles panel shows the Box Model with CSS properties. The Console at the bottom shows some errors/warnings. The selected element is the "Sign up" button link.

Ez clap! Here's what we accomplished:

1. ✅ **Opened Safari** - was already running
2. ✅ **Navigated to github.com** - GitHub homepage loaded with the hero text and navigation
3. ✅ **Opened Web Inspector on an element** - Right-clicked the "Sign up" button and selected "Inspect Element". The Web Inspector panel is now showing:
   - **Elements tab** with the full HTML DOM tree, highlighting the `<a>` tag for the Sign up link
   - **Computed styles** on the right showing CSS properties like `color`, `white-space`, `border-radius`
   - **Box Model** visualization showing padding, border, and margin values
   - **Console** at the bottom with some page errors/warnings

The Web Inspector is your best friend for debugging web pages - you can see the HTML structure, CSS styles, network requests, and way more. Nailed it! 💪

The Quick Look preview is now displaying the markdown file with all its content about Indian political trajectory. You can see the full text rendered right there without needing to open any app. Quick Look is honestly one of macOS's best features - just select any file and hit Space. Ez clap! 🔥

To close the preview, just press Space again or click the X button in the top-left corner of the preview.

results.instruction: Open TextEdit, create a document with a bulleted list of 5 items, change font to Helvetica 14pt
Open TextEdit in plain text mode and type a simple Python script

input.text:
# Simple Python Script
# A basic calculator program

def add(a, b):
    return a + b

def subtract(a, b):
    return a - b

def multiply(a, b):
    return a * b

def divide(a, b):
    if b == 0:
        return "Error: Division by zero"
    return a / b

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("Simple Calculator")
    print("-" * 20)
    
    x = 10
    y = 5
    
    print(f"{x} + {y} = {add(x, y)}")
    print(f"{x} - {y} = {subtract(x, y)}")
    print(f"{x} * {y} = {multiply(x, y)}")
    print(f"{x} / {y} = {divide(x, y)}")
    
    print("\nDone!")

input.command:
cat << 'PYTHON_SCRIPT' | pbcopy
# Simple Python Script
# A basic calculator program

def add(a, b):
    return a + b

def subtract(a, b):
    return a - b

def multiply(a, b):
    return a * b

def divide(a, b):
    if b == 0:
        return "Error: Division by zero"
    return a / b

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("Simple Calculator")
    print("-" * 20)
    
    x = 10
    y = 5
    
    print(f"{x} + {y} = {add(x, y)}")
    print(f"{x} - {y} = {subtract(x, y)}")
    print(f"{x} * {y} = {multiply(x, y)}")
    print(f"{x} / {y} = {divide(x, y)}")
    
    print("\nDone!")
PYTHON_SCRIPT

results.instruction: Open a PNG image in Preview and rotate it 90 degrees clockwise

input.command: PNG=$(ls ~/Desktop/*.png | head -1) && open -a Preview "$PNG" && echo "Opened successfully"

results.finalText:
LETS GOOO! 🎉 The image has been rotated 90 degrees clockwise! You can see it clearly - all the text that was horizontal is now rotated sideways, and the title bar shows "Screenshot 2026-03-21 at 12.44.29 AM... — Edited" confirming the rotation was applied. The "Edited" tag means Preview recognized the change.

CHECKPOINT [2]: Successfully rotated the PNG image 90 degrees clockwise in Preview. The title bar now shows "Edited" confirming the rotation was applied.
SCENE: Preview app showing the rotated PNG screenshot - all content is now rotated 90 degrees clockwise, and the title bar shows "Edited" status.

CHECKPOINT [3]: Task complete - PNG image opened in Preview and rotated 90 degrees clockwise.

Nailed it! 🔥 Here's what I did:
1. **Found a PNG** on your Desktop (`Screenshot 2026-03-21 at 12.44.29 AM.png`)
2. **Opened it in Preview** using the `open -a Preview` command
3. **Rotated it 90° clockwise** using Tools → Rotate Right (⌘R)

The image is now rotated and marked as "Edited" in Preview. If you want to save the rotation, just hit ⌘S, or if you want to undo it, ⌘Z will bring it back to normal. Ez clap! 💪

```bash
grep -r -i "capybara" ~/ --include="*.txt" --include="*.sh" --include="*.py" --include="*.md" 2>/dev/null
```

**What it did:**
- 🔍 `-r` — searched **recursively** through all subdirectories
- 🔡 `-i` — **case-insensitive** matching (catches "Capybara", "CAPYBARA", etc.)

```
HTTP/2 200
date: Fri, 03 Apr 2026 16:57:32 GMT
content-type: text/html
server: cloudflare
last-modified: Tue, 24 Mar 2026 22:07:32 GMT
allow: GET, HEAD
accept-ranges: bytes
age: 6425
cf-cache-status: HIT
cf-ray: 9e699ce8f98d85a0-HKG
```

Some juicy details from those headers:

- **HTTP/2 200** — site's up and healthy, responding with 200 OK ✅
- **server: cloudflare** — example.com is sitting behind Cloudflare's CDN, interesting!
- **content-type: text/html** — yep, it's a webpage
- **cf-cache-status: HIT** — Cloudflare served this from cache (not directly from origin), hence the `age: 6425` (cached for ~1.7 hours)
- **allow: GET, HEAD** — only GET and HEAD methods are allowed
- **last-modified: Tue, 24 Mar 2026** — page was last updated recently

ez clap 🤙 Done and done!

results.instruction: Open Terminal and write a Python script that reads a CSV file and prints the first 5 rows

input.command:
# Create a sample CSV file
cat > ~/sample_data.csv << 'EOF'
Name,Age,City,Occupation,Salary
Alice,30,New York,Engineer,95000
Bob,25,San Francisco,Designer,78000
Charlie,35,Chicago,Manager,110000
Diana,28,Austin,Developer,88000
Eve,32,Seattle,Analyst,82000
Frank,40,Boston,Director,130000
Grace,27,Denver,Marketing,72000
Henry,33,Miami,Sales,68000
Iris,29,Portland,Data Scientist,92000
Jack,38,Atlanta,Consultant,105000
EOF

echo "CSV file created!"
cat ~/sample_data.csv

input.text:
python3 -c "
import csv

filename = 'sample_data.csv'

print('Reading CSV file:', filename)
print('-' * 50)

with open(filename, newline='') as csvfile:
    reader = csv.DictReader(csvfile)
    for i, row in enumerate(reader):
        if i >= 5:
            break
        print(f'Row {i+1}: {dict(row)}')

print('-' * 50)
print('Done! First 5 rows printed.')
"

cat > ~/read_csv.py << 'SCRIPT'
import csv
import sys

def read_csv_first_n_rows(filename, n=5):
    """Read a CSV file and print the first n rows."""
    print(f"Reading CSV file: {filename}")
    print("-" * 50)

    with open(filename, newline="") as csvfile:
        reader = csv.DictReader(csvfile)
        for i, row in enumerate(reader):
            if i >= n:
                break
            print(f"Row {i+1}: {dict(row)}")

    print("-" * 50)
    print(f"Done! First {n} rows printed.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    filename = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "sample_data.csv"
    read_csv_first_n_rows(filename)
SCRIPT

results.instruction: Open Terminal and use awk to process a text file, printing the second column

input.text:
cat > /tmp/sample_data.txt << 'EOF'
Alice 28 Engineer New_York
Bob 34 Designer Chicago
Carol 25 Developer Austin
Dave 41 Manager Seattle
Eve 30 Analyst Boston
Frank 27 DevOps Denver
EOF
echo "=== File Contents ===" && cat /tmp/sample_data.txt && echo "" && echo "=== AWK: Print 2nd Column (Age) ===" && awk '{print $2}' /tmp/sample_data.txt

results.finalText:
CHECKPOINT [2]: AWK successfully processed the file and printed the second column! 

SCENE: Terminal showing the full file contents, then the awk output — second column (ages: 28, 34, 25, 41, 30, 27) perfectly extracted from the data.

LETS GOOO! That worked perfectly! 🎉 Here's a breakdown of what just happened:

---

## ✅ What We Did

### 📄 Created `/tmp/sample_data.txt` with 4 columns:
```
Alice  28  Engineer  New_York
Bob    34  Designer  Chicago
Carol  25  Developer Austin
Dave   41  Manager   Seattle
Eve    30  Analyst   Boston
Frank  27  DevOps    Denver
```

### 🔧 Ran `awk '{print $2}'` on it:
```
28
34
25
41
30
27
```

---

## 💡 How `awk` works here

| Part | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `awk` | The text processing beast |
| `'{print $2}'` | For every line, print field #2 (space-delimited by default) |
| `/tmp/sample_data.txt` | The input file |

Awk uses `$1`, `$2`, `$3`... to reference columns. `$0` would print the whole line. You can also do stuff like `awk '{print $1, $3}'` to print multiple columns, or `awk -F','` to use a comma as the delimiter for CSV files. Awk is lowkey one of the most powerful one-liners in the unix toolbox — absolute classic! 🚀

results.instruction: Open Terminal and write a shell script that monitors CPU usage every 5 seconds for 30 seconds

input.text:
cat > ~/cpu_monitor.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
# CPU Monitor Script - checks CPU usage every 5 seconds for 30 seconds

echo "=== CPU Monitor Started ==="
echo "Monitoring CPU every 5 seconds for 30 seconds..."
echo ""

for i in $(seq 1 6); do
  TIMESTAMP=$(date "+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
  CPU_USAGE=$(top -l 1 -s 0 | grep "CPU usage" | awk '{print $3}' | tr -d '%')
  echo "[$TIMESTAMP] Reading $i/6 - CPU Usage: ${CPU_USAGE}% user"
  if [ $i -lt 6 ]; then
    sleep 5
  fi
done

echo ""
echo "=== Monitoring Complete ==="
EOF

results.finalText:
**LETS GOOOOO!! 🎉 EZ CLAP!** Script ran perfectly to completion! All 6 readings done, terminal is back to idle prompt!

CHECKPOINT [3]: Script ran to completion — all 6 CPU readings captured, "=== Monitoring Complete ===" printed, back at idle prompt.
SCENE: Terminal showing full script output with 6 CPU readings and "=== Monitoring Complete ===" at the bottom, prompt back to `(venv) nivesh@niveshdeMacBook-Pro ~ %`.

---

Here's the full breakdown of what went down:

### ✅ CPU Monitoring Script — Complete!

**Script saved to:** `~/cpu_monitor.sh`

| Reading | Timestamp | CPU Usage |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| 1/6 | 2026-04-04 01:09:27 | **12.12%** user |
| 2/6 | 2026-04-04 01:09:32 | **4.6%** user |
| 3/6 | 2026-04-04 01:09:38 | **1.65%** user |
| 4/6 | 2026-04-04 01:09:43 | **3.47%** user |
| 5/6 | 2026-04-04 01:09:48 | **12.71%** user |
| 6/6 | 2026-04-04 01:09:53 | **4.91%** user |

The script:
- ✅ Ran every **5 seconds** for **30 seconds total** (6 readings × 5s)
- ✅ Showed **timestamped CPU usage** using macOS's `top` command
- ✅ Printed a clean start/finish banner

Your machine's CPU was pretty chill the whole time — mostly under 5%, with a couple spikes up to ~12%. Healthy system, my dude! 🐾

Region,Salesperson,Product,Quarter,Units,Revenue

North,Alice,Widget B,Q1,85,8500
North,Bob,Widget A,Q1,95,9500

South,Dave,Widget B,Q1,90,9000

East,Eve,Widget B,Q2,175,17500
East,Frank,Widget A,Q2,160,16000
East,Frank,Widget B,Q1,140,14000

West,Henry,Widget B,Q1,105,10500

# Also check System Applications

results.instruction: Take a screenshot using Cmd+Shift+3, then open it in Preview and crop it

input.command:
# Let's check what display we have and try xdotool or import
which import 2>/dev/null && echo "import available" || echo "no import"
which xdotool 2>/dev/null && echo "xdotool available" || echo "no xdotool"
# Check if there are any existing screenshots
ls -la ~/Desktop/Screen* 2>/dev/null
ls -la ~/Desktop/*.png 2>/dev/null
# Also check the default screenshot location
defaults read com.apple.screencapture location 2>/dev/null || echo "default location"

# Let me try using the Screenshot app via applescript
osascript -e 'do shell script "screencapture -x ~/Desktop/my_screenshot.png"' 2>&1

# Let me try with DISPLAY set or check if we're on an actual display
echo $DISPLAY
# Try with -D flag to specify display
screencapture -D 1 -x ~/Desktop/my_screenshot.png 2>&1

# Check if there's a newer screenshot that just appeared (from our Cmd+Shift+3)
ls -lt ~/Desktop/Screenshot* 2>/dev/null | head -5
ls -lt ~/Desktop/Screenshots/Screenshot* 2>/dev/null | head -5
